It underpins both inventory accuracy (a warehouse holding 500 cases of chicken needs to know the true total pounds on hand, not an assumed average) and correct invoicing (the customer is billed for what was actually shipped, not a catalog estimate). Structurally it follows the Catch-Weight Data Model, carrying both count and actual weight as independent, equally necessary fields.

fields.name: Unit / case identifier
fields.description: The specific packaged unit this weight reading applies to.

fields.name: Nominal count
fields.description: The catalog/planned count for the unit (e.g. one case).

fields.name: Actual (catch) weight
fields.description: The measured weight captured at the scale, which may diverge from the nominal/catalog weight.

fields.name: Capture timestamp / location
fields.description: When and at which point in packaging the weight was captured.

fields.name: Lot reference
fields.description: The production lot the unit belongs to, tying the weight reading back to the batch.

2e49adf0-dbf5-4625-bfda-b538e7085483.name: Catch-Weight Data Model
2e49adf0-dbf5-4625-bfda-b538e7085483.lifecycle: Defines how a variable-weight unit is represented so a single fixed quantity field (what most standard ERP/WMS modules assume) doesn't lose information. Every catch-weight unit carries two independent quantities that must both be tracked and both flow through to invoicing: count (1 case, 1 each) and actual weight (e.g. 42.3 lb), plus the unit of measure the weight is captured in. Nominal/catalog weight (the average or list weight used for planning and initial pricing estimates) is stored separately from actual weight (the real, measured value for that specific unit) — the two are expected to diverge and inventory/financial calculations must use actual, not nominal. Variable-measure trade items use a GTIN with a distinct prefix (not scanned at retail POS like a fixed-quantity GTIN), carried with GS1-128 Application Identifier (01) for the GTIN plus AI (310n)/(320n) for net weight in kg/lb — the standard GS1 mechanism for carrying actual weight from the scale through label, shipping, and invoicing without re-keying. A pure count-based ERP/WMS integration built against this data model needs a parallel weight field wherever quantity is used — receiving, inventory, pricing, and shipping — not just at the point of sale.
2e49adf0-dbf5-4625-bfda-b538e7085483.description: The Catch-Weight Data Model is the data structure specification that makes catch-weight tracking possible in systems (ERP, WMS) that were originally built around a single fixed-quantity field. It formalizes the principle that a variable-weight unit needs two independent numbers tracked together at every step — how many units there are, and how much they actually weigh — plus a clear separation between the nominal/catalog weight used for upfront planning and pricing estimates and the actual weight measured for that specific unit, since the two are expected to diverge and financial/inventory calculations must always use actual. It also specifies how that actual weight travels through barcoding and EDI using the GS1 variable-measure GTIN and GS1-128 Application Identifiers, so the weight captured at the scale survives all the way to invoicing without being re-keyed by hand. Any system integration project touching receiving, inventory, pricing, or shipping for catch-weight products has to be built against this model, not the simpler fixed-quantity assumption most standard ERP/WMS modules ship with by default.

fields.name: Count
fields.description: The number of discrete units (1 case, 1 each) — the fixed-quantity dimension most standard ERP/WMS fields already track.

fields.name: Actual weight
fields.description: The real, measured weight of this specific unit, captured at the scale; diverges from nominal weight and is what inventory and invoicing must use.

fields.name: Nominal/catalog weight
fields.description: The average or list weight used for upfront planning and initial pricing estimates before the actual unit is measured.

fields.name: Unit of measure
fields.description: The unit (kg, lb) the weight is captured in, carried alongside the value.

fields.name: GS1 variable-measure GTIN
fields.description: A distinct GTIN prefix range for variable-measure trade items, not scanned like a fixed-quantity retail GTIN.

fields.name: GS1-128 AI (310n)/(320n)
fields.description: The Application Identifier fields that carry net weight in kg or lb from the scale through label, shipping, and invoicing data.

a7ea6ca7-6b6e-4101-beff-e581956d8a36.name: Package Integrity / Foreign Material Inspection Record
a7ea6ca7-6b6e-4101-beff-e581956d8a36.lifecycle: Generated per inspection event (metal detector, X-ray) at packaging; rejections logged and disposed per procedure.
a7ea6ca7-6b6e-4101-beff-e581956d8a36.description: This record is the logged output of an inline physical-hazard inspection event — a metal detector or X-ray system flagging (or clearing) a unit as it moves through packaging. Every pass through the detector generates a pass/reject outcome; the record captures which unit was affected, which piece of equipment made the call, and what happened to a rejected unit (segregated for investigation, scrapped, or in some cases visually confirmed as a false trigger and returned to the line). It's the evidence trail behind what's typically a designated CCP: a facility running metal detection or X-ray as its physical-hazard critical control point needs this record to demonstrate the CCP was actually monitored at the required frequency, not just that the equipment was present and turned on.

fields.name: Inspection event ID
fields.description: Unique identifier for this pass through the detector, tying the record to a specific unit and moment.

fields.name: Equipment identifier
fields.description: Which metal detector or X-ray unit generated the pass/reject outcome.

fields.name: Unit / lot reference
fields.description: The specific unit or lot that was inspected.

fields.name: Pass/reject outcome
fields.description: The detector's determination for this unit.

fields.name: Disposition of rejects
fields.description: What happened to a rejected unit — segregated for investigation, scrapped, or confirmed as a false trigger and returned to the line.

fields.name: Timestamp
fields.description: When the inspection event occurred, needed to demonstrate the CCP was monitored at the required frequency.

6425e8df-c318-495b-ae4e-a3a81af541f1.name: Label Copy Draft
6425e8df-c318-495b-ae4e-a3a81af541f1.lifecycle: Drafted from the BOM and marketing input; revised through QA/regulatory review before artwork.
6425e8df-c318-495b-ae4e-a3a81af541f1.description: The Label Copy Draft is the working, pre-approval version of everything that will eventually appear on a package's label — ingredient statement, allergen declaration, Nutrition Facts, any nutrient-content or marketing claims, net weight statement — assembled from the current Bill of Materials/Recipe plus marketing's brand and claims input. It's the document that gets iterated on before anything reaches artwork: QA and regulatory review it against the formulation and applicable labeling regulations, sending it back for revision whenever the ingredient list, allergen statement, or a claim doesn't match what the current recipe actually supports. Only once it clears that review does it become the source document that a Label Compliance Checklist is run against and that design turns into finished Label Artwork.

fields.name: Ingredient statement (draft)
fields.description: The draft ingredient list assembled from the current Bill of Materials/Recipe.

fields.name: Allergen declaration (draft)
fields.description: The draft "Contains" statement listing major allergens per the current formulation.

fields.name: Nutrition Facts (draft)
fields.description: Draft nutrient values calculated from the formulation, pending final verification.

fields.name: Claims
fields.description: Any nutrient-content or marketing claims proposed for the label, subject to regulatory review.

fields.name: Net weight statement
fields.description: The declared net contents for the package.

fields.name: Revision status
fields.description: Where the draft stands in QA/regulatory review — pending, sent back for revision, or cleared to proceed to compliance checklist and artwork.

3afd5ba3-988d-462f-836b-b99ec6c661c2.name: Label Compliance Checklist
3afd5ba3-988d-462f-836b-b99ec6c661c2.lifecycle: Completed during allergen/claims verification. Each line item on the checklist corresponds to one rule in the Label Compliance Check Catalog (see that artifact), which is what makes the checklist a candidate for automated tooling rather than manual review.
3afd5ba3-988d-462f-836b-b99ec6c661c2.description: The Label Compliance Checklist is the completed, per-label evidence that allergen declarations and any claims on a Label Copy Draft were actually verified against the formulation and applicable regulations before the label moved forward. Each line item corresponds directly to one rule defined in the Label Compliance Check Catalog — an allergen cross-check against the ingredient list, a nutrient-content claim's numeric threshold, a required structure/format element like minimum type size — so completing the checklist means working through that catalog's rule set for this specific label rather than a free-form review. Because each item maps to a concrete, comparable check, this is the artifact singled out as a strong candidate for automated tooling: a system that already has the formulation and label copy as structured data can run most of these checks itself rather than requiring a person to manually re-verify ingredients against a printed label draft.

fields.name: Checklist line item
fields.description: One specific rule being verified, corresponding to a single entry in the Label Compliance Check Catalog.

fields.name: Rule reference
fields.description: Pointer to the specific catalog rule this line item checks (e.g. an allergen cross-check, a nutrient-content claim threshold, a minimum type-size requirement).

fields.name: Pass/fail result
fields.description: Whether this label copy draft satisfies the referenced rule.

fields.name: Reviewer
fields.description: Who performed the verification for this line item.

fields.name: Date completed
fields.description: When this checklist item was verified.

d1139caf-37bd-4f97-9220-08feba95bdce.name: Label Compliance Check Catalog
d1139caf-37bd-4f97-9220-08feba95bdce.lifecycle: Maintained by regulatory/QA; extended when a new claim type or allergen regulation is adopted. Enumerates the specific, automatable checks behind allergen/claims verification, each expressed as a comparison between two existing data sources rather than a free-text review: (1) Allergen cross-check — every major allergen present in the Material/Product Specification's ingredient list for the formulation must appear in the label's "Contains" statement, and nothing in the Contains statement may be absent from the ingredients (catches both under- and over-declaration); (2) Claim threshold checks — nutrient-content claims are checked against their FDA-defined numeric threshold, using the calculated Nutrition Facts values for the formulation: "good source of [nutrient]" claims (e.g. fiber) under 21 CFR 101.54 (10–19% Daily Value per serving), "low fat" under 21 CFR 101.62 (≤3g total fat per RACC), and "reduced sodium" under 21 CFR 101.61 (≥25% less sodium than a named reference food, with the reference food declared) — 101.13 provides the general nutrient-content-claim framework these specific sections build on; (3) Structure/format checks — allergen statement placement, type size minimums, and required statements (e.g. gluten-free labeling's <20ppm attestation under 21 CFR 101.91) are present per the applicable regulation for the claim(s) made. A failing check blocks label approval and routes back to formulation or label copy rather than to print; a passing run against all applicable rules is what allows the checklist to close without manual re-review of ingredients the system already had structured data for.
d1139caf-37bd-4f97-9220-08feba95bdce.description: The Label Compliance Check Catalog is the versioned rule set that turns allergen and claims label review from a subjective manual read-through into a defined list of specific, repeatable comparisons. Each entry names two existing data sources and the exact comparison to run between them — for example, comparing every major allergen present in the formulation's ingredient list (from the Material/Product Specification) against the label's Contains statement, in both directions, so both under-declaration (a missed allergen) and over-declaration (a Contains statement claiming an allergen not actually present) are caught. Regulatory/QA maintains it, adding entries whenever a new claim type is adopted or an allergen regulation changes (e.g. the FASTER Act adding sesame as a major allergen). It's the reference a Label Compliance Checklist is built from, and because each rule is expressed as a concrete data comparison rather than free text, it's what makes automated label-compliance checking possible instead of requiring a person to manually cross-reference ingredients against label copy every time.

fields.name: Allergen cross-check
fields.description: Every major allergen present in the Material/Product Specification's ingredient list must appear in the label's Contains statement, and nothing in the Contains statement may be absent from the ingredients — catching both under- and over-declaration.

fields.name: Claim threshold checks
fields.description: Nutrient-content claims checked against their FDA-defined numeric threshold using calculated Nutrition Facts values: e.g. "good source of [nutrient]" under 21 CFR 101.54 (10-19% DV/serving), "low fat" under 21 CFR 101.62 (≤3g total fat per RACC), "reduced sodium" under 21 CFR 101.61 (≥25% less sodium than a named reference food).

fields.name: Structure/format checks
fields.description: Allergen statement placement, minimum type size, and required statements (e.g. gluten-free's <20ppm attestation under 21 CFR 101.91) present per the applicable regulation for the claim(s) made.

68a4cc0a-b5c0-4ba4-91de-332c4c74c684.name: Label Artwork File
68a4cc0a-b5c0-4ba4-91de-332c4c74c684.lifecycle: Produced by design agency from approved label copy; goes through final approval before print; version-controlled to prevent obsolete artwork use.
68a4cc0a-b5c0-4ba4-91de-332c4c74c684.description: The Label Artwork File is the finished, print-ready design output produced by a design agency (or in-house design team) once a Label Copy Draft has cleared QA/regulatory review and the Label Compliance Checklist. It translates approved text content — ingredient statement, allergen declaration, Nutrition Facts, claims, net weight — into the actual visual layout, typography, and brand design that will be printed. Because a printer running an obsolete version can put an out-of-compliance or simply outdated label into the market, this file is kept under strict version control with a clear approval gate before it's released to print; a change anywhere upstream (a recipe change, a claims revision) requires a new artwork revision to flow back through approval rather than being patched directly onto the print file.

f6d69f97-18d8-4a0f-b4fb-77c9320edb1d.name: FSIS Label Submission
f6d69f97-18d8-4a0f-b4fb-77c9320edb1d.lifecycle: Submitted to FSIS for meat/poultry products. Since 2013, and further expanded by a 2023 final rule (88 FR 2798), most meat/poultry labels are self-certified under generic label approval — FSIS pre-approval is now required only for temporary approvals, religious exempt product labels, and labels bearing special statements/claims (e.g. natural, certified gluten-free, health claims, negative/ingredient-absence claims not otherwise generically approvable), not as a default step for the great majority of labels.
f6d69f97-18d8-4a0f-b4fb-77c9320edb1d.description: The FSIS Label Submission is the regulatory filing pathway meat/poultry processors use before a label can be applied to product leaving the plant. Historically every label needed FSIS pre-approval; that changed under the agency's generic label approval program, first expanded in 2013 and further broadened by a 2023 final rule. Today the label copy team (working from the Label Copy Draft and the underlying BOM/Recipe) self-certifies that a label meets FSIS's generic requirements, and submits to FSIS for actual review only in narrow cases: temporary approvals, religious exempt product labels, or labels carrying special statements or claims the agency hasn't pre-cleared generically (e.g. "natural," certified gluten-free, health claims, or claims that an ingredient/allergen is absent). For the majority of routine labels, this step is now a self-certification record-keeping action rather than a submit-and-wait regulatory gate, which materially changes the label-approval timeline compared to FDA-regulated (non-meat/poultry) products that have no equivalent submission step at all.

fields.name: Submission type
fields.description: Temporary approval, religious exempt, or special-statement/claim submission — the only categories FSIS still reviews directly.

fields.name: Claim/statement basis
fields.description: The specific claim or statement (e.g. 'natural', gluten-free, negative claim) triggering the need for FSIS review rather than self-certification.

fields.name: Generic approval determination
fields.description: The processor's own record of whether the label qualifies for self-certified generic approval, and the basis for that determination.

ffb44422-779f-4bc8-86be-ff6fa6d73d68.name: Lot / Date Code Record
ffb44422-779f-4bc8-86be-ff6fa6d73d68.lifecycle: Generated at lot coding, encoding production date/shift/line; becomes the join key for all downstream traceability records for that lot.
ffb44422-779f-4bc8-86be-ff6fa6d73d68.description: The Lot / Date Code Record captures the specific alphanumeric code physically applied to product at lot coding — typically encoding production date, shift, and line — the moment a batch of finished product becomes individually traceable in downstream systems. Every record that needs to reference "which specific production run" (traceability queries, mock recalls, customer complaint investigation, shelf-life calculation) joins back to this code rather than to the more abstract Production/Work Order, because the work order can span multiple lots while the lot code identifies one specific run's output. It's generated automatically by the line's coder/printer (ink-jet, laser, or thermal transfer) reading from the active Production/Work Order and current date/time, and it becomes the primary key that the Batch/Production Record, Shipping Record, and any Traceability Lot Code Record are ultimately keyed against for that unit of product.

fields.name: Lot/date code value
fields.description: The actual alphanumeric code applied to product, typically encoding production date, shift, and line.

fields.name: Production date
fields.description: The date of production encoded in the code.

fields.name: Shift / line
fields.description: The shift and production line the code identifies, distinguishing this run's output from others on the same date.

fields.name: Source work order reference
fields.description: The Production/Work Order the coder read from when generating this code.

fields.name: Coding equipment identifier
fields.description: Which line coder/printer (ink-jet, laser, or thermal transfer) generated the code.

f2777655-3ff5-43b8-9e51-494f0704b2a6.name: Label Application Verification Record
f2777655-3ff5-43b8-9e51-494f0704b2a6.lifecycle: Generated per in-line vision-system check confirming correct label/lot code applied to the correct product.
f2777655-3ff5-43b8-9e51-494f0704b2a6.description: The Label Application Verification Record is the automated in-line check confirming that the label physically applied to a package matches the product actually running on the line, and that its printed lot/date code is present and legible. A camera-based vision system positioned after the labeler reads the label (via OCR/OCV against the expected text, or barcode/GTIN decode) and compares it against the current Production/Work Order's expected product identity before the unit is allowed to proceed downstream — catching the specific, high-consequence failure mode of the wrong label ending up on the wrong product (an undeclared-allergen risk, not just a cosmetic defect). A mismatch triggers an automatic reject/divert rather than a manual stop, and the record itself becomes evidence, alongside the Lot / Date Code Record, that the correct label was verified in place before the product left the line.

fields.name: Expected identity
fields.description: Product/label identity pulled from the active Production/Work Order that the vision system checks against.

fields.name: Read result
fields.description: What the vision system actually decoded from the applied label (text, barcode, or lot code).

fields.name: Pass/fail disposition
fields.description: Match or mismatch outcome, and the automatic action taken (pass-through vs. reject/divert).

70dd593f-d5f5-4ef5-8e57-decefe3ba0a5.name: Shelf-Life / Remaining Shelf-Life Record
70dd593f-d5f5-4ef5-8e57-decefe3ba0a5.lifecycle: Calculated from production/expiration dates; checked against customer minimum-remaining-shelf-life requirements at shipment.
70dd593f-d5f5-4ef5-8e57-decefe3ba0a5.description: The Shelf-Life / Remaining Shelf-Life Record tracks how much usable life a specific lot has left as of any given point in its journey — calculated as the gap between the product's fixed expiration/best-by date (set at formulation from stability testing) and the current date or, more commonly in B2B food distribution, the date the product is expected to reach a specific customer. Many retail and foodservice customers contractually require a minimum percentage of shelf life remaining at delivery (a common practice sometimes called a "code date" or "freshness" requirement) — a lot that would arrive under that threshold gets diverted to a different customer, discounted, or written off rather than shipped to a buyer who would reject it on arrival. This record is what the warehouse/shipping planning step actually checks before allocating a specific lot to a specific outbound order, working hand-in-hand with FEFO-driven Inventory Location Record picking logic.

fields.name: Lot reference
fields.description: The specific lot this shelf-life calculation applies to.

fields.name: Production / expiration date
fields.description: The fixed dates set at formulation from stability testing that anchor the shelf-life window.

fields.name: Calculation reference date
fields.description: The current date or expected delivery date used to compute remaining shelf life.

fields.name: Remaining shelf life
fields.description: The computed gap between the reference date and the expiration date, typically expressed as days or percentage of total shelf life remaining.

fields.name: Customer minimum-remaining-shelf-life requirement
fields.description: The threshold the destination customer contractually requires at delivery, checked against the remaining shelf life before allocation.

fields.name: Allocation outcome
fields.description: Whether the lot was allocated to this order, diverted to a different customer, discounted, or written off.

551a4e09-160c-4f45-ac51-2000370afcab.name: Shipping Record
551a4e09-160c-4f45-ac51-2000370afcab.lifecycle: Created at outbound shipment; records lot(s), quantity, and destination — Shipping is one of the Critical Tracking Events (CTEs) defined at 21 CFR 1.1340 under FSMA 204. Its required fields are defined by the Carrier Handoff KDE Set (see that artifact), which is what the plant must actually capture at trailer loading to keep the trace record continuous once custody passes to the carrier.
551a4e09-160c-4f45-ac51-2000370afcab.description: The Shipping Record is the plant's evidence that an outbound shipment happened and, under FSMA 204, is also the documentation of Shipping — one of the rule's Critical Tracking Events (CTEs) for foods on the Food Traceability List. It's created at the dock as product is loaded onto the outbound trailer, capturing which lot(s) went out, in what quantity, and to which destination. For foods in FSMA 204 scope, the specific data the record must capture isn't left to the plant's discretion — it's defined by the Carrier Handoff KDE Set (the Key Data Elements 21 CFR 1.1340 requires for the Shipping CTE), which is what actually drives what fields this record needs to contain versus what a plant might capture purely for its own operational purposes (e.g. internal routing, carrier scheduling). Once the KDE set is captured correctly at this step, the plant's traceability obligation for that shipment is discharged — continuity of the trace record past this point depends on the receiving party's own Receiving CTE, not on anything the shipping plant does afterward.

fields.name: Traceability lot code(s)
fields.description: The lot code(s) shipped, carried forward from the Traceability Lot Code Record — a required Shipping KDE under the Carrier Handoff KDE Set.

fields.name: Product description and quantity/UOM
fields.description: What was shipped and how much, in a stated unit of measure — required Shipping KDEs.

fields.name: Ship-to / ship-from location description
fields.description: Origin and destination location descriptions — required Shipping KDEs, identifier format (e.g. GS1 GLN) optional.

fields.name: Date shipped
fields.description: The date the shipment left the shipper's control — a required Shipping KDE.

fields.name: Reference document type/number
fields.description: The shipping document (e.g. bill of lading, ASN) tying the physical shipment to this record — a required Shipping KDE.

b5e32a3a-afdb-4264-a583-9bfcf21d6999.name: Carrier Handoff KDE Set
b5e32a3a-afdb-4264-a583-9bfcf21d6999.lifecycle: Defines the specific Key Data Elements 21 CFR 1.1340 requires the shipper to record for the Shipping Critical Tracking Event, since this is the plant's last touchpoint before the trace record depends on a party outside its direct control: traceability lot code(s), product description, quantity and unit of measure, ship-to (recipient) location description, ship-from location description, date shipped, traceability lot code source information, and reference document type/number. GS1 identifiers (GTIN for product, GLN for locations) are a permitted implementation choice for expressing these descriptions where the receiver can translate them, not an FDA-mandated format — the rule itself is identifier-format-agnostic. Transporter identity is explicitly NOT a required KDE: §1.1340 excludes the transporter from the recipient-location KDE by definition, and the rule imposes no separate requirement to capture transporter identity. For cold-chain product, trailer temperature at load (see Temperature Record) is still worth capturing alongside as the plant's own food-safety evidence of condition at handoff, but it is a plant record, not an FSMA 204 KDE. Once the required fields are captured and the Shipping Record is created, continuity of the trace record downstream depends on the receiving party's own Receiving CTE — the plant's obligation is fully discharged by capturing its own KDE set correctly, not by tracking what happens after handoff.
b5e32a3a-afdb-4264-a583-9bfcf21d6999.description: The Carrier Handoff KDE Set formalizes exactly what 21 CFR 1.1340 requires a shipper to record for the Shipping Critical Tracking Event under FSMA 204's Food Traceability Rule — it exists as its own artifact, distinct from the Shipping Record it populates, because this is the last point the plant directly controls before the trace record depends on a party (the carrier, then the receiver) outside its four walls. The rule specifies the required fields but is deliberately identifier-format-agnostic: a plant can express product and location identity using GS1 GTINs/GLNs, or any other identifier scheme the receiving party can translate, as an implementation choice rather than an FDA mandate. One frequently misunderstood point this KDE set makes explicit: transporter identity is not a required field at all — §1.1340 excludes the carrier from the recipient-location KDE by definition, so a plant does not need to capture who is hauling the load to satisfy this rule (though it may do so for its own logistics purposes).

fields.name: Traceability lot code(s)
fields.description: The FSMA 204 lot code(s) on the shipment, carried forward from the TLC Record.

fields.name: Product description
fields.description: What is being shipped, described sufficiently for the receiver to identify it.

fields.name: Quantity and unit of measure
fields.description: How much of the product shipped, in a stated UOM.

fields.name: Ship-to location description
fields.description: Where the shipment is going — the recipient location.

fields.name: Ship-from location description
fields.description: Where the shipment originated — this plant's location.

fields.name: Date shipped
fields.description: The date the shipment left the shipper's control.

fields.name: Traceability lot code source information
fields.description: Reference identifying where/how the traceability lot code was established.

fields.name: Reference document type/number
fields.description: The shipping document (e.g. bill of lading, ASN) type and number tying the physical shipment to this record.

9cc2d1ff-a495-474c-bc2d-5615fe7e94bb.name: Traceability Lot Code (TLC) Record
9cc2d1ff-a495-474c-bc2d-5615fe7e94bb.lifecycle: A new TLC is assigned only at initial packing of a raw agricultural commodity (other than seafood), first land-based receiving of a food from a fishing vessel, or transformation, per 21 CFR 1.1320 — it must NOT be reassigned at other CTEs like shipping, and must be maintained and passed forward through every subsequent shipment.
9cc2d1ff-a495-474c-bc2d-5615fe7e94bb.description: The Traceability Lot Code (TLC) Record captures the single identifier that FSMA 204 uses to tie every downstream record for a given lot of a Food Traceability List item back to its origin. What makes this record structurally different from an ordinary internal Lot / Date Code Record is the strict rule about when a new code may be assigned: only at initial packing of a raw agricultural commodity (other than seafood), at the first land-based receiving of a food from a fishing vessel, or at transformation (e.g. a change in the food's form, such as cooking, combining ingredients, or repackaging). At every other Critical Tracking Event — receiving, shipping — the existing TLC must be carried forward unchanged, not reassigned; a plant that mistakenly generates a new TLC at, say, shipping breaks the chain a downstream investigator would need to trace the product back to its origin. This is why the record exists independently of the more general internal Lot/Date Code Record: the TLC has its own assignment rule that internal coding conventions are not required to follow.

fields.name: Traceability lot code (TLC)
fields.description: The FSMA 204 lot identifier assigned to the food, carried forward unchanged through every subsequent Critical Tracking Event.

fields.name: Assignment event
fields.description: Which of the three permitted assignment points generated this TLC — initial packing of a raw agricultural commodity (non-seafood), first land-based receiving from a fishing vessel, or transformation.

fields.name: Assignment date and location
fields.description: When and where the TLC was assigned, needed as traceability lot code source information for downstream KDE sets.

fields.name: Food Traceability List item reference
fields.description: The specific FTL-covered food this TLC applies to, since the rule's TLC obligations apply only to items on that list.

1188e42e-3097-4203-b657-0f88020f34f0.name: EPCIS Event
1188e42e-3097-4203-b657-0f88020f34f0.lifecycle: Emitted at supply-chain touchpoints (what/where/when/why — EPCIS's standard four core dimensions); GS1 US has published explicit guidance mapping EPCIS 2.0 onto FDA's FSMA 204 CTEs/KDEs, making it an industry-promoted, emerging de facto format for sharing traceability data across company boundaries — not itself an FDA-mandated format.
1188e42e-3097-4203-b657-0f88020f34f0.description: An EPCIS Event is a standardized traceability record — one instance of GS1's "what/where/when/why" data model — that captures a single supply-chain occurrence (a shipment leaving a dock, a case being received, a transformation happening) in a structured, machine-readable form designed to be exchanged across company boundaries. It matters to this domain because GS1 US has published explicit guidance mapping EPCIS 2.0's event structure onto FDA's FSMA 204 Critical Tracking Events and Key Data Elements, positioning EPCIS as the emerging de facto industry format for actually transmitting KDE data (from the Carrier Handoff KDE Set, the Traceability Lot Code Record, etc.) between trading partners electronically — but it's important that this is industry-promoted infrastructure, not an FDA-mandated format; a plant can satisfy FSMA 204 using EPCIS, EDI, or any other means capable of producing and sharing the required records.

fields.name: What
fields.description: The object(s) the event concerns — e.g. the traceability lot code or GTIN identifying the product/case involved.

fields.name: Where
fields.description: The location(s) involved in the event — read point and, where relevant, business location, expressible as a GS1 GLN or other identifier.

fields.name: When
fields.description: The event time and record time, distinguishing when the occurrence happened from when it was captured.

fields.name: Why
fields.description: The business context of the event — e.g. shipping, receiving, or a transformation step — mapped to the corresponding FSMA 204 Critical Tracking Event.

4c3d5e6f-7a8b-4c9d-0e1f-2a3b4c5d6e7a.name: Case/Pack Configuration Verification Record
4c3d5e6f-7a8b-4c9d-0e1f-2a3b4c5d6e7a.lifecycle: Generated per case or pallet configuration event during primary/secondary packaging; verifies case count, case labeling, and outer-carton weight/date coding before the unit proceeds to package integrity check.
4c3d5e6f-7a8b-4c9d-0e1f-2a3b4c5d6e7a.description: This record captures the verification step that happens as primary consumer-facing units are grouped into secondary packaging (cases, trays, shrink-wrapped multi-packs): confirming the case holds the correct unit count for its configuration, that outer-carton labeling (lot/date code, case GTIN) matches what's inside, and that the packed case's weight falls within expected range for a case of that size. It sits between the primary-package fill/seal step and the downstream package integrity check, catching configuration errors (wrong case count, mismatched labeling) before a unit reaches physical-hazard inspection. Unlike the Catch Weight Record (which verifies the primary unit's own net contents) or the Package Integrity / Foreign Material Inspection Record (which screens for physical hazards), this record is specifically about whether the secondary packaging itself was assembled correctly.

fields.name: Case/pallet configuration ID
fields.description: Identifies the specific case or pallet-build event being verified.

fields.name: Expected unit count
fields.description: The case configuration's specified number of primary units per case.

fields.name: Actual unit count (verified)
fields.description: The count confirmed present in the case at verification.

fields.name: Case label / lot-date code match
fields.description: Confirmation that the outer-carton label's lot and date coding matches the units packed inside.

fields.name: Case weight (verified)
fields.description: The packed case's measured weight, checked against expected range for that configuration.

fields.name: Disposition
fields.description: Pass, or the corrective action taken for a mismatched/miscounted case (rework, hold, reject).

5d4e6f7a-8b9c-4d0e-1f2a-3b4c5d6e7f8b.name: FEFO Exception/Override Log
5d4e6f7a-8b9c-4d0e-1f2a-3b4c5d6e7f8b.lifecycle: Generated whenever a WMS-directed FEFO pick is manually overridden (e.g. a closer-to-expiry pallet is inaccessible or damaged); logged alongside the routine FEFO pick transactions the WMS already records.
5d4e6f7a-8b9c-4d0e-1f2a-3b4c5d6e7f8b.description: A WMS enforcing FEFO picking logic directs which specific lot/pallet should ship next based on expiration date, but real warehouse conditions occasionally force a departure from that system-directed sequence — a closer-to-expiry pallet blocked by rack access, damaged packaging discovered at pick time, or a customer-specific shelf-life requirement that calls for a later-expiring lot instead. This log captures those departures: which pick was overridden, the reason, who authorized it, and which lot actually shipped instead. It exists because a pure success-path FEFO transaction log doesn't show when and why the system's own rotation logic wasn't followed, which is exactly the information a shelf-life or recall investigation would need if a downstream complaint traces back to an unexpectedly old lot in a shipment.

fields.name: Override event ID
fields.description: Unique identifier for this departure from system-directed FEFO picking.

fields.name: System-directed lot/pallet
fields.description: The lot the WMS would have picked under standard FEFO logic.

fields.name: Actual lot/pallet shipped
fields.description: The lot actually picked and shipped instead.

fields.name: Reason for override
fields.description: Why the system-directed pick wasn't followed (access issue, damage, customer requirement, etc.).

fields.name: Authorized by
fields.description: Who approved the departure from standard FEFO logic.

fields.name: Timestamp
fields.description: When the override occurred.

7e7db815-8cfb-429d-be62-3f35e49fd6e3.name: Preventive Maintenance Record
7e7db815-8cfb-429d-be62-3f35e49fd6e3.lifecycle: Generated per scheduled or unscheduled maintenance event on production/CCP equipment (retort, oven, filler, cooker, metal detector, refrigeration compressor) — captures what was serviced, when, by whom, and whether the equipment was returned to a verified operating state before production resumed.
7e7db815-8cfb-429d-be62-3f35e49fd6e3.description: This record documents mechanical/electrical upkeep on production and CCP-critical equipment — distinct from a calibration record, which verifies a measurement instrument's accuracy against a reference. A Preventive Maintenance Record instead tracks belt replacement, seal inspection, compressor service, and similar work that keeps equipment mechanically capable of holding the process parameters a CCP depends on in the first place. A missed or overdue maintenance interval on CCP-critical equipment is itself a food-safety-relevant finding, since equipment failure mid-run is a well-established root cause behind CCP deviations.

fields.name: Equipment identifier
fields.description: The specific piece of production or CCP-critical equipment serviced.

fields.name: Maintenance type (scheduled/unscheduled)
fields.description: Whether this was a planned PM interval or an unscheduled repair triggered by a fault or failure.

fields.name: Work performed
fields.description: What was actually serviced, replaced, or repaired.

fields.name: Date and technician
fields.description: When the work occurred and who performed it — internal maintenance staff or an outside contractor.

fields.name: Return-to-service verification
fields.description: Confirmation the equipment was checked and capable of holding required process parameters before production resumed, since equipment failure is a common CCP-deviation root cause.

fields.name: Next scheduled PM date
fields.description: When the next planned maintenance interval falls due.

2d8b96d5-f9ba-4de5-a60b-0da0f60afaec.name: Training / Qualification Record
2d8b96d5-f9ba-4de5-a60b-0da0f60afaec.format: data record, per employee per training event
2d8b96d5-f9ba-4de5-a60b-0da0f60afaec.lifecycle: Created when an employee completes required training for a role-specific duty — CCP monitoring, allergen changeover/line clearance verification, or batching/weighing against a scaled BOM — and renewed on the facility's own recertification cadence or whenever a process/procedure the employee is qualified on changes materially.
2d8b96d5-f9ba-4de5-a60b-0da0f60afaec.description: This record is the evidence that a person performing a competency-gated duty was actually trained and verified capable of it, not just assigned the task. FSMA PCHF requires individuals performing preventive-control-related duties to be qualified through training or experience, with the PCQI training requirement for the food safety plan itself being the narrowest, most explicit case; this dataset extends the same documented-competency principle to the operator-level duties already present here (CCP monitoring, line clearance, batching) as a reasonable design choice rather than a direct per-role mandate. In practice, this is exactly the kind of record a GFSI/HACCP audit samples — an auditor asking to see evidence an operator performing a CCP check was actually trained to recognize a deviation, not just handed the responsibility.

fields.name: Employee identifier
fields.description: The individual who received the training or qualification.

fields.name: Role/duty trained for
fields.description: The specific task the training qualifies the employee to perform, e.g. CCP monitoring, line clearance verification, batching.

fields.name: Training date and method
fields.description: When the training occurred and how it was delivered — classroom, on-the-job, online module.

fields.name: Trainer/qualifying authority
fields.description: Who conducted or verified the training.

fields.name: Competency verification result
fields.description: How the employee's competency was confirmed — written test, observed demonstration, or supervisor sign-off.

fields.name: Recertification due date
fields.description: When this qualification must be renewed, per the facility's own retraining cadence.
